input_text:
Voyager 1 embarked on its mission from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. Although both probes were launched using a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, Voyager 1's launch occurred sixteen days after that of Voyager 2, despite the specific trajectories mandated by NASA. The primary objective of this undertaking was to observe for a duration of five years, during which both Jupiter and Saturn would be studied in detail. The infrequent yet favorable cosmic alignment of these distant planets, which happens approximately every 176 years, was a rare and welcome occurrence.

Voyager 1 arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this visit, the probe sent back the first high-resolution imagery of Jupiter's faint rings and documented active volcanism on its moon Io, thereby setting the initial record for volcanism outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 executed its closest approach to Saturn. It gathered extremely detailed data about Saturn’s ring system and successfully photographed Titan. This transit by Saturn propelled Voyager 1 beyond the solar system’s plane, concluding its planetary tour and setting a trajectory toward interstellar space.

Following a suggestion offered by astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 directed its instruments toward Earth on February 14, 1990, transmitting one final picture while it was roughly 6 billion kilometers distant. This singular image, which appeared as a single pixel against the sunlight, became known as the Pale Blue Dot. This transmission was the last photograph sent by the spacecraft before the cameras were shut down to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, upon crossing the heliopause boundary, making it the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated based on Sagan’s advice. This record includes greetings in 55 languages, along with visual and musical content intended for any civilization that might eventually encounter it. Its power supply comes from three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238; the steady decay of this isotope, which has a half-life of 87.7 years, provides the necessary power.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is positioned about 25 billion kilometers from Earth while maintaining a speed nearing 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will attain the significant milestone of having traveled a distance equal to one light-day.
output_text:
Voyager 1 began its mission from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. Although both probes utilized a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket for launch, Voyager 1’s deployment occurred sixteen days subsequent to that of Voyager 2, despite the specific flight paths assigned by NASA. The primary goal of this endeavor was to conduct observations for a period of five years, during which both Jupiter and Saturn would be scrutinized in depth. This alignment of the distant planets, occurring roughly every 176 years, was a rare and welcome celestial event.

Voyager 1 reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this transit, the probe relayed the first high-resolution images of Jupiter's faint rings and documented Io's active volcanism, thus establishing the initial record of volcanism outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 performed its closest pass to Saturn. It collected highly detailed data concerning Saturn's ring system and successfully photographed Titan. This journey past Saturn propelled Voyager 1 beyond the solar system's confines, concluding its planetary tour and setting a course into interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, transmitting one final photograph while it was approximately 6 billion kilometers distant. This solitary image, appearing as a single pixel against the sunlight, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This transmission marked the last photograph transmitted by the spacecraft before the cameras were deactivated to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, upon crossing the heliopause boundary, making it the inaugural human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated based on Sagan’s advice. This record contains greetings in 55 languages, alongside audiovisual material intended for any civilization that might eventually find it. Its power supply is derived from three radioisotope thermoelectric generators fueled by plutonium-238; the steady decay of this isotope, which possesses a half-life of 87.7 years, provides the requisite energy.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated about 25 billion kilometers from Earth while maintaining a velocity approaching 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will reach the important benchmark of having traveled a distance equivalent to one light-day.
